title: Listening - Part 3: Enjoying a Book
description: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a book they recently enjoyed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for enjoying the book. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.
instructions: You are going to hear five extracts in which people talk about a book they recently enjoyed. For questions 19–23, choose from the list A–H the main reason each speaker gives for enjoying the book. Use the letters once only. There are three letters that you do not need to use.

extracts.context: Five people talk about a book they have recently read.
extracts.audio_script: Speaker 1 Woman: Historical novels don’t usually interest me because I expect them to feel like lessons with fictional characters added. A friend gave me one set in London during the Second World War. [19]Without filling pages with facts, it showed how ordinary people managed rationing, air raids and separation from their families, and I finished it feeling I understood that period far better.[/19] The plot was exciting too, but what stayed with me was how much I had learned without making any special effort.||Speaker 2 Man: I read a mystery novel on holiday and thought I’d solved it by about halfway through. The author seemed to leave very obvious clues about which character was responsible. [20]Then, in the final chapters, a small detail from the beginning became important and the truth was completely different from anything I had predicted.[/20] I actually went back to check that the clues were there, and they were. It's rare for a book to surprise me so successfully, so I’ve recommended it to everyone.||Speaker 3 Woman: The novel I read is about three friends leaving school and trying to decide what to do next. Not much happens in terms of action, so some readers might find it slow. [21]I loved it because the characters worried about the same sorts of decisions as my friends and I do, and their conversations sounded like people I could genuinely know.[/21] I cared about their choices because they never seemed perfect or invented simply to make the story more dramatic.||Speaker 4 Man: I love reading, but recently I've been travelling to college, working some evening shifts and doing assignments, so I never seem to have a long quiet afternoon. I chose a collection of linked stories rather than a full-length novel. [22]Each section could be read in twenty minutes on the train, yet the stories connected together, so the book fitted easily into my schedule while still feeling satisfying.[/22] For once, I reached the end before forgetting what happened at the beginning.||Speaker 5 Woman: I picked up a travel book about a coastal walking route because its cover looked attractive. I wasn't planning a holiday and expected simply to enjoy the photographs. [23]But the writer described fishing villages, cliff paths and hidden beaches so vividly that I began looking up train times and have now arranged to spend a weekend walking part of the route myself.[/23] A book has never before made me book accommodation. I hope the weather is as good as it looked in the photographs.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_1
dialogue.text: Historical novels don’t usually interest me because I expect them to feel like lessons with fictional characters added. A friend gave me one set in London during the Second World War. Without filling pages with facts, it showed how ordinary people managed rationing, air raids and separation from their families, and I finished it feeling I understood that period far better. The plot was exciting too, but what stayed with me was how much I had learned without making any special effort.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_2
dialogue.text: I read a mystery novel on holiday and thought I’d solved it by about halfway through. The author seemed to leave very obvious clues about which character was responsible. Then, in the final chapters, a small detail from the beginning became important and the truth was completely different from anything I had predicted. I actually went back to check that the clues were there, and they were. It's rare for a book to surprise me so successfully, so I’ve recommended it to everyone.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_3
dialogue.text: The novel I read is about three friends leaving school and trying to decide what to do next. Not much happens in terms of action, so some readers might find it slow. I loved it because the characters worried about the same sorts of decisions as my friends and I do, and their conversations sounded like people I could genuinely know. I cared about their choices because they never seemed perfect or invented simply to make the story more dramatic.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_4
dialogue.text: I love reading, but recently I've been travelling to college, working some evening shifts and doing assignments, so I never seem to have a long quiet afternoon. I chose a collection of linked stories rather than a full-length novel. Each section could be read in twenty minutes on the train, yet the stories connected together, so the book fitted easily into my schedule while still feeling satisfying. For once, I reached the end before forgetting what happened at the beginning.

dialogue.speaker: speaker_5
dialogue.text: I picked up a travel book about a coastal walking route because its cover looked attractive. I wasn't planning a holiday and expected simply to enjoy the photographs. But the writer described fishing villages, cliff paths and hidden beaches so vividly that I began looking up train times and have now arranged to spend a weekend walking part of the route myself. A book has never before made me book accommodation. I hope the weather is as good as it looked in the photographs.

options.text: It reminded them of their childhood.
It taught them about a past period.
Its structure suited their busy routine.
It was recommended by a famous person.
It encouraged them to visit somewhere.

questions.speaker: Speaker 1
questions.explanation: The speaker valued the insight the novel provided into everyday life during a historical period (evidence [19]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 2
questions.explanation: The speaker particularly enjoyed being surprised by the solution at the end of the mystery (evidence [20]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 3
questions.explanation: The speaker identified with characters who behaved and spoke in a realistic way (evidence [21]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 4
questions.explanation: The book's short connected sections worked well with the speaker's limited reading time (evidence [22]).

questions.speaker: Speaker 5
questions.explanation: The descriptions in the book led the speaker to arrange a trip to the place described (evidence [23]).
